subject: Physics
q: The relative error in the determination of the surface area of a sphere is $\alpha$. Then the relative error in the determination of its volume is
question: The relative error in the determination of the surface area of a sphere is $\alpha$. Then the relative error in the determination of its volume is
solution:
Relative error in Surface area, $\frac{\Delta \mathrm{s}}{\mathrm{s}}=2 \times \frac{\Delta \mathrm{r}}{\mathrm{r}}=\alpha$ and relative error in volume, $\frac{\Delta \mathrm{v}}{\mathrm{v}}=3 \times \frac{\Delta \mathrm{r}}{\mathrm{r}}$
$\therefore$ Relative error in volume w.r.t. relative error in area,
$$
\frac{\Delta \mathrm{v}}{\mathrm{v}}=\frac{3}{2} \alpha
$$

x=acos⁡ωt

y=asin⁡ωt

and z=aωt

The speed of the particle is:
question:
The position co-ordinates of a particle moving in a 3D coordinate system is given by

x=acos⁡ωt

y=asin⁡ωt

and z=aωt

The speed of the particle is:
solution:
x=acos⁡ωt

y=asin⁡ωt

z=a ωt

vx=-aωsin⁡ωt

vy=aωcos⁡ωt

v2=aω

v=vx2+vy2+vz2

=-aωsin⁡ωt2+aωcos⁡ωt2+aω2

=aω2

q: The acceleration due to gravity is found up to an accuracy of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo>%</mo></math> on a planet. The energy supplied to a simple pendulum of known mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>m</mi></math> to undertake oscillations of time period <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>T</mi></math> is being estimated. If time period is measured to an accuracy of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo>%</mo></math>, the accuracy to which <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>E</mi></math> is known as ________<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>%</mo></math>
question: The acceleration due to gravity is found up to an accuracy of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo>%</mo></math> on a planet. The energy supplied to a simple pendulum of known mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>m</mi></math> to undertake oscillations of time period <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>T</mi></math> is being estimated. If time period is measured to an accuracy of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo>%</mo></math>, the accuracy to which <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>E</mi></math> is known as ________<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>%</mo></math>
solution:
T=2πlg

Δll×100=Δgg×100+2ΔTT×100

⇒Δll×100=4+2×3=10%

E=12mω2A2=12mglA2Therefore, ΔEE×100=Δgg×100+Δll×100=4+10=14%

question: The relative error in the determination of the surface area of a sphere is <math> <mi>α</mi> </math>. The relative error in the determination of its volume is

q: A physical quantity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math> is given as <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mrow><msup><mi>a</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><msup><mi>b</mi><mn>3</mn></msup></mrow><mrow><mi>c</mi><msqrt><mi>d</mi></msqrt></mrow></mfrac></math>. The percentage error in the measurement of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>a</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>b</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>c</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>d</mi></math> are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>%</mo><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>2</mn><mo>%</mo><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>3</mn><mo>%</mo></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo>%</mo></math> respectively. The percentage error in the measurement of quantity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math> will be
question: A physical quantity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math> is given as <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mrow><msup><mi>a</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><msup><mi>b</mi><mn>3</mn></msup></mrow><mrow><mi>c</mi><msqrt><mi>d</mi></msqrt></mrow></mfrac></math>. The percentage error in the measurement of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>a</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>b</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>c</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>d</mi></math> are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>%</mo><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>2</mn><mo>%</mo><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>3</mn><mo>%</mo></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo>%</mo></math> respectively. The percentage error in the measurement of quantity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math> will be
solution:
The given quantity is P=a2b3cd.

The percentage error for a quantity can be given by the formula ∆xx×100.

Relation of the relative errors will be,

ΔPP=2Δaa+3Δbb+Δcc+12Δdd

Hence, the percentage error is calculated by substituting the values,

ΔPP×100=2×1+3×2+3+12×4=13%.

subject: Physics
q:
For the four sets of three measured physical quantities as given below. Which of the following options is correct?
i A1=24.36,B1=0.0724,C1=256.2
ii A2=24.44,B2=16.082,C2=240.2
iii A3=25.2,B3=19.2812,C3=236.183
iv A4=25,B4=236.191,C4=19.5
question:
For the four sets of three measured physical quantities as given below. Which of the following options is correct?
i A1=24.36,B1=0.0724,C1=256.2
ii A2=24.44,B2=16.082,C2=240.2
iii A3=25.2,B3=19.2812,C3=236.183
iv A4=25,B4=236.191,C4=19.5
solution:
In addition or subtractions, final answer must contain the equal numbers of digits after decimal as the adding or subtracting quantities among which is having the lowest numbers of digits after decimal.

A1+B1+C1=24.36+0.0724+256.2=280.6324=280.6

The Least number of  significant digit after decimal  in the above expression is 1 So, Sum should conatian 1 significant digit after decimal.
A2+B2+C2=24.44+16.082+240.2=280.722=280.7

The Least number of  significant digit after decimal  in the above expression is 1 So, Sum should conatian 1 significant digit after decimal
A3+B3+C3=25.2+19.2812+236.183=280.6642=280.7

The Least number of  significant digit after decimal  in the above expression is 1 So, Sum should conatian 1 significant digit after decimalA4+B4+C4=25+236.191+19.5=280.691=281

The Least number of  significant digit after decimal  in the above expression is 0 So, Sum should conatian 0 significant digit after decimal
Answer should be A1+B1+C1<A2+B2+C2=A3+B3+C3<A4+B4+C4

subject: Physics
q: The amount of heat produced in an electric circuit depends upon the current $(I)$, resistance $(R)$ and time $(t)$. If the error made in the measurements of the above quantities are $2 \%, 1 \%$ and $1 \%$ respectively then the maximum possible error in the total heat produced will be
question: The amount of heat produced in an electric circuit depends upon the current $(I)$, resistance $(R)$ and time $(t)$. If the error made in the measurements of the above quantities are $2 \%, 1 \%$ and $1 \%$ respectively then the maximum possible error in the total heat produced will be
solution:
Given : $\frac{\Delta I}{I} \times 100=2 \%$;
$\frac{\Delta R}{R} \times 100=1 \%$ and $\frac{\Delta t}{t} \times 100=1 \%$
Heat produced, $H=I^2 R t$
Maximum possible error in heat produced,
$$
\begin{aligned}
& =\frac{\Delta H}{H} \times 100 \\
& =2\left(\frac{\Delta I}{I} \times 100\right)+\frac{\Delta R}{R} \times 100+\frac{\Delta t}{t} \times 100 \\
& =2 \times 2 \%+1 \%+1 \%=6 \%
\end{aligned}
$$

subject: Physics
q: A particle is moving eastwards with a velocity of $5 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ in 10 seconds the velocity changes to $5 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ northwards. The average acceleration in this time is
question: A particle is moving eastwards with a velocity of $5 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ in 10 seconds the velocity changes to $5 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ northwards. The average acceleration in this time is
solution: $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}}=\frac{\overrightarrow{\mathrm{V}}_{\mathrm{f}}-\overrightarrow{\mathrm{V}}_{\mathrm{i}}}{\mathrm{t}}$ $=\frac{5 \hat{\mathrm{j}}-5 \hat{\mathrm{i}}}{10}=\frac{1}{2}(\hat{\mathrm{j}}-\hat{\mathrm{i}})$ $\therefore \mathrm{a}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \mathrm{~ms}^{-2}$ towards north west

=PQsinθ

subject: Physics
q: Two resistance are given as R1=(10±0.5) Ω and R2=(15±0.5) Ω. The percentage error in the measurement of equivalent resistance when they are connected in parallel is
question: Two resistance are given as R1=(10±0.5) Ω and R2=(15±0.5) Ω. The percentage error in the measurement of equivalent resistance when they are connected in parallel is
solution:
For a parallel combination the equivalent resistance is 

1R=1R1+1R2   ...(i)

A general expression for error can be written as x±∆x

Differentiating equation (i)

dRR2=dR1R12+dR2R22   ...(ii)

The equivalent resistance with R1=10 Ω & R2=15 Ω is

1R=110+115

⇒R=15025=6Ω

Substituting the above value in equation (ii)

dR=360.5100+0.515×15=0.26

⇒R=6±0.26

Hence, the percentage error is 

dRR×100=0.266×100=4.33%

q: If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi>P</mi><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>=</mo><mn>3</mn><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><msqrt><mn>3</mn></msqrt><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">j</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mn>2</mn><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><mo>^</mo></mover></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi>Q</mi><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>=</mo><mn>4</mn><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><msqrt><mn>3</mn></msqrt><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">j</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mn>2</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><mo>^</mo></mover></math>  then, the unit vector in the direction of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi>P</mi><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>×</mo><mover><mi>Q</mi><mo>→</mo></mover></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mi>x</mi></mfrac><mo>(</mo><msqrt><mn>3</mn></msqrt><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">j</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn><msqrt><mn>3</mn></msqrt><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>)</mo></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is
question: If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi>P</mi><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>=</mo><mn>3</mn><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><msqrt><mn>3</mn></msqrt><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">j</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mn>2</mn><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><mo>^</mo></mover></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi>Q</mi><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>=</mo><mn>4</mn><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><msqrt><mn>3</mn></msqrt><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">j</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mn>2</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><mo>^</mo></mover></math>  then, the unit vector in the direction of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi>P</mi><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>×</mo><mover><mi>Q</mi><mo>→</mo></mover></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mi>x</mi></mfrac><mo>(</mo><msqrt><mn>3</mn></msqrt><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">j</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn><msqrt><mn>3</mn></msqrt><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>)</mo></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is
solution:
Unit vector in the direction of P→×Q→ is n^=P→×Q→|P→×Q→|.

Here, P→×Q→=i^j^k^332432.5=3i^2+j^2-3k^

And P→×Q→=322+122+32=4=2

⇒P→×Q→|P→×Q→|=123i^2+j^2-3k^

=14(3i^+j^-23k^)  ⇒x=4

q: If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi>A</mi><mo>→</mo></mover></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi>B</mi><mo>→</mo></mover></math> are two vectors satisfying the relation <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi>A</mi><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>·</mo><mover><mi>B</mi><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>=</mo><mo>|</mo><mover><mi>A</mi><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>×</mo><mover><mi>B</mi><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>|</mo></math>. Then the value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>|</mo><mover><mi>A</mi><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>-</mo><mover><mi>B</mi><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>|</mo></math> will be:
question: If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi>A</mi><mo>→</mo></mover></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi>B</mi><mo>→</mo></mover></math> are two vectors satisfying the relation <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi>A</mi><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>·</mo><mover><mi>B</mi><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>=</mo><mo>|</mo><mover><mi>A</mi><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>×</mo><mover><mi>B</mi><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>|</mo></math>. Then the value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>|</mo><mover><mi>A</mi><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>-</mo><mover><mi>B</mi><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>|</mo></math> will be:
solution:
A→·B→=|A→×B→|
ABcosθ=ABsinθ⇒θ=45°
|A→-B→|=A2+B2-2ABcos45°
=A2+B2-2AB
Hence option (4).

subject: Physics
q: The maximum error in the measurement of resistance, current and time for which current flows in an electrical circuit are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>%</mo><mo>,</mo><mn>2</mn><mo>%</mo></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo>%</mo></math> respectively. The maximum percentage error in the detection of the dissipated heat will be:
question: The maximum error in the measurement of resistance, current and time for which current flows in an electrical circuit are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>%</mo><mo>,</mo><mn>2</mn><mo>%</mo></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo>%</mo></math> respectively. The maximum percentage error in the detection of the dissipated heat will be:
solution:
Heat generated through resistance is given by, H=i2Rt

Therefore, the percentage error in the calculation can be found by,

⇒ΔHH×100=2×Δii×100+ΔRR×100+Δtt×100

⇒ΔHH×100=4%+1%+3%

⇒ΔHH×100=8%

q: In order to determine the Young's Modulus of a wire of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> (measured using a scale of least count <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>001</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math>) and length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>  (measured using a scale of least count <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi></math>), a weight of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> (measured using a scale of least count <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> ) was hanged to get the elongation of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> (measured using a scale of least count <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>001</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math>). What will be the fractional error in the value of Young's Modulus determined by this experiment?
question: In order to determine the Young's Modulus of a wire of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> (measured using a scale of least count <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>001</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math>) and length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>  (measured using a scale of least count <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi></math>), a weight of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> (measured using a scale of least count <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> ) was hanged to get the elongation of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> (measured using a scale of least count <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>001</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math>). What will be the fractional error in the value of Young's Modulus determined by this experiment?
solution:
Y=StressStrain=FLAl=mgLπR2l

ΔYY=Δmm+ΔLL+2ΔRR+Δll

ΔYY×100=10011000+11000+20.0010.2+0.0010.5

=110+110+1+15=1410=1.4%

question: If two vectors <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi>A</mi><mo>→</mo></mover></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi>B</mi><mo>→</mo></mover></math> having equal magnitude <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> are inclined at an angle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>θ</mi></math>, then
solution:
The magnitude of resultant vector A→+B→ is given by, 

R'=A2+B2+2ABcosθ

Here A=B=R

Then R'=R2+R2+2R2cosθ

=R21+cosθ

=2R2cos2θ2

=2Rcosθ2

subject: Physics
q: An experiment is performed to obtain the value of acceleration due to gravity g by using a simple pendulum of length L. In this experiment time for 100 oscillations is measured  by using a watch of 1 second least count and the value is 90.0 seconds. The length L is measured by using a meter scale of least count 1 mm and the value is 20.0 cm. The error in the determination of g would be :
question: An experiment is performed to obtain the value of acceleration due to gravity g by using a simple pendulum of length L. In this experiment time for 100 oscillations is measured  by using a watch of 1 second least count and the value is 90.0 seconds. The length L is measured by using a meter scale of least count 1 mm and the value is 20.0 cm. The error in the determination of g would be :
solution:
T = 2 π L g

∴    T 2 = 4 π 2 L g

∴     g = 4 π 2 L T 2

% Error in g = Δ L L 0 × 1 0 0 + 2 Δ T T 0 × 1 0 0

                   = 1 × 1 0 - 3 0.20 × 1 0 0 + 2 1 9 0 × 1 0 0

                   = 0.5 + 2 1 0 9

                   = 0.5 + 2 0 9

                   = 0.5 + 2.22

                  = 2.7%

question:
Three students S1, S2 and S3 perform an experiment for determining the acceleration due to gravity (g) using a simple pendulum. They use different lengths of pendulum and record time for different number of oscillations. The observations are as shown in the table.

Student No. Length of pendulum
(cm)		 Number of oscillations
(n)		 Total time for
n oscillations		 Time
period
(s)
1. 64.0 8 128.0 16.0
2. 64.0 4 64.0 16.0
3. 20.0 4 36.0 9.0

(Least count of length =0.1 m, least count for time =0.1 s)

If E1, E2 and E3 are the percentage errors in g for students 1, 2 and 3, respectively, then the minimum percentage error is obtained by student no          .
solution:
T=2πℓg⇒g=4π2ℓT2
Δgg=Δℓℓ+2∆TT

ΔT= leastcountoftime ΔT0 numberofoscillations (n) 
Δgg=Δℓℓ+2ΔT0nT

As Δℓ and ΔT0 are same for all observations so
Δgg is minimum for highest value of ℓ, n and T

⇒ Minimum percentage error in g is for student number 1.

q: The density of a material, in the shape of a cube, is determined by measuring three sides of the cube and its mass. If the relative errors in measuring the mass and length are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo>%</mo></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>%</mo></math>, respectively, the maximum error in determining the density is:
question: The density of a material, in the shape of a cube, is determined by measuring three sides of the cube and its mass. If the relative errors in measuring the mass and length are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo>%</mo></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>%</mo></math>, respectively, the maximum error in determining the density is:
solution:
ρ=ML3⇒Δρρ=ΔMM+3ΔLL⇒Δρρ×100=1.5+3×1
% relative error,=4.5 %

subject: Physics
q: Which of the following relations is true for two unit vector <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi>A</mi><mo>^</mo></mover></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi>B</mi><mo>^</mo></mover></math> making an angle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>θ</mi></math> to each other?
question: Which of the following relations is true for two unit vector <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi>A</mi><mo>^</mo></mover></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi>B</mi><mo>^</mo></mover></math> making an angle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>θ</mi></math> to each other?
solution:
We know,

 1- cos θ=2sin2θ21+ cos θ=2cos2θ2

Now,a→+b→·a→+b→=a→2+2a→·b→+b→2⇒a→+b→·a→+b→=12+2×1×1×cosθ+12 ⇒a→+b→·a→+b→=21+cosθ⇒a→+b→2=4cos2θ2⇒a→+b→=2cosθ2  ...(1)

Similarly,

a→-b→·a→-b→=a→2-2a→·b→+b→2⇒a→-b→·a→-b→=12-2×1×1×cosθ+12⇒a→-b→·a→-b→=21-cosθ⇒a→-b→2=4sin2θ2⇒a→-b→=2sin θ2  ...(2)

Therefore,

A^-B^=A^+B^tanθ2

q: A cylindrical wire of mass<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo> </mo><mo>(</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mo>±</mo><mo> </mo><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>01</mn><mo>)</mo><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> has length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mn>8</mn><mo> </mo><mo>±</mo><mo> </mo><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>04</mn><mo>)</mo><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> and radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mn>6</mn><mo> </mo><mo>±</mo><mo> </mo><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>03</mn><mo>)</mo><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi></math>. The maximum error in its density will be
question: A cylindrical wire of mass<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo> </mo><mo>(</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mo>±</mo><mo> </mo><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>01</mn><mo>)</mo><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> has length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mn>8</mn><mo> </mo><mo>±</mo><mo> </mo><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>04</mn><mo>)</mo><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> and radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mn>6</mn><mo> </mo><mo>±</mo><mo> </mo><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>03</mn><mo>)</mo><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi></math>. The maximum error in its density will be
solution:
The formula to calculate the density of the material of the wire is given by

ρ=MπR2L   ...1

Hence, the formula to calculate the percentage error in calculating the density of the material can be written as

dρρ×100=dMM+2dRR+dLL×100   ...2

Substitute the values of the known parameters into equation (2) to calculate the required percentage error.

=0.010.4+2×0.036+0.048×100=4%

question: Let <math><mfenced close="|" open="|" separators="|"><mrow><mover accent="true"><mrow><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow></msub></mrow><mo>→</mo></mover></mrow></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mn>3</mn><mo>,</mo><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mfenced close="|" open="|" separators="|"><mrow><mover accent="true"><mrow><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub></mrow><mo>→</mo></mover></mrow></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mn>5</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi></math> and <math><mfenced close="|" open="|" separators="|"><mrow><mover accent="true"><mrow><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow></msub></mrow><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mover accent="true"><mrow><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub></mrow><mo>→</mo></mover></mrow></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mn>5</mn><mo>.</mo></math> The value of <math><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mn>2</mn><mover accent="true"><mrow><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow></msub></mrow><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mn>3</mn><mover accent="true"><mrow><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub></mrow><mo>→</mo></mover></mrow></mfenced><mo>∙</mo><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mn>3</mn><mover accent="true"><mrow><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow></msub></mrow><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn><mover accent="true"><mrow><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub></mrow><mo>→</mo></mover></mrow></mfenced></math> is:
solution:
A→1+A→2=5
A→1+A→22=25
A→12+A→22+2A→1A→2=25
9+25+2A→1∙A→2=25
A→1.A→2=-92
Now, 2A→1+3A→2∙3A→1-2A→2=6A→12-4A→1A→2+9A→1A→2-6A→22
=6.A→12+5A→1A→2-6A→22
=6×9+5-92-6×25
=54-452-150
= -118.5

The magnitude of a coplanar vector <math><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>C</mi></mrow><mo>→</mo></mover></math> such that <math><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>A</mi></mrow><mo>→</mo></mover><mi> </mi><mo>.</mo><mi> </mi><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>C</mi></mrow><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>=</mo><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>B</mi></mrow><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>.</mo><mi> </mi><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>C</mi></mrow><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>=</mo><mi> </mi><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>A</mi></mrow><mo>→</mo></mover><mi> </mi><mo>.</mo><mi> </mi><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>B</mi></mrow><mo>→</mo></mover></math> is given by:
solution:
If C→=ai^ + bj^ then, 
A→ . C→=A→ . B→  
a+b=1  .......(i)
B→. C→= A→ . B→
2a-b=1  ......(ii)
solving equations (i) and (ii) we get, 
a=13, b=23
C→= 19+49= 59

q: If two vectors <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi>P</mi><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>=</mo><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mn>2</mn><mi>m</mi><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">j</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mi>m</mi><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><mo>^</mo></mover></math>  and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi>Q</mi><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>=</mo><mn>4</mn><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">j</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mi>m</mi><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><mo>^</mo></mover></math>  are perpendicular to each other. Then, the value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>m</mi></math> will be
question: If two vectors <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi>P</mi><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>=</mo><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mn>2</mn><mi>m</mi><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">j</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mi>m</mi><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><mo>^</mo></mover></math>  and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi>Q</mi><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>=</mo><mn>4</mn><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">j</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mi>m</mi><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><mo>^</mo></mover></math>  are perpendicular to each other. Then, the value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>m</mi></math> will be
solution:
For two vectors to be perpendicular, their dot product is equal to zero.

So, P→·Q→=0

⇒i^+2mj^+mk^·4i^-2j^+mk^=0⇒4-4m+m2=0⇒m-22=0⇒m=2

subject: Physics
q: The density $\rho$ of a uniform cylinder is determined by measuring its mass $m$, length $l$ and diameter $d$. The measured values of $m$, $l$ and $d$ are $97.42 \pm 0.02$ g, $8.35 \pm 0.05$ mm and $20.20 \pm 0.02$ mm, respectively. Calculated percentage fractional error in $\rho$ is _______.
question: The density $\rho$ of a uniform cylinder is determined by measuring its mass $m$, length $l$ and diameter $d$. The measured values of $m$, $l$ and $d$ are $97.42 \pm 0.02$ g, $8.35 \pm 0.05$ mm and $20.20 \pm 0.02$ mm, respectively. Calculated percentage fractional error in $\rho$ is _______.
solution:
The density of a uniform cylinder is given by $\rho = \dfrac{m}{V} = \dfrac{4m}{\pi d^2 l}$

The percentage fractional error in density is given by:

$\dfrac{\Delta \rho}{\rho} \times 100 = \left( \dfrac{\Delta m}{m} + 2\dfrac{\Delta d}{d} + \dfrac{\Delta l}{l} \right) \times 100$

Substituting the given values:

$\dfrac{\Delta \rho}{\rho} \times 100 = \left( \dfrac{0.02}{97.42} + 2 \times \dfrac{0.02}{20.20} + \dfrac{0.05}{8.35} \right) \times 100$

$\dfrac{\Delta \rho}{\rho} \times 100 = \left( 0.000205 + 0.00198 + 0.005988 \right) \times 100$

$\dfrac{\Delta \rho}{\rho} \times 100 = 0.008173 \times 100 = 0.8173\%$

Rounding off to two decimal places, the percentage error is $0.82\%$.

Answer: $0.82\%$

subject: Physics
q: A physical quantity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi></math> is represented by the formula <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><msup><mi>m</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><msup><mi>r</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>4</mn></mrow></msup><msup><mrow><mo mathvariant="italic"> </mo><mi mathvariant="italic">g</mi></mrow><mi mathvariant="italic">x</mi></msup><msup><mi>l</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mfrac><mn>3</mn><mn>2</mn></mfrac></mrow></msup></math> If the percentage errors found in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi><mo mathvariant="italic">,</mo><mo mathvariant="italic"> </mo><mi>m</mi><mo mathvariant="italic">,</mo><mo mathvariant="italic"> </mo><mi>r</mi><mo mathvariant="italic">,</mo><mo mathvariant="italic"> </mo><mi>l</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>g</mi></math> are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>18</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>1</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>4</mn></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>p</mi></math> respectively, then find the value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>p</mi></math>.
question: A physical quantity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi></math> is represented by the formula <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><msup><mi>m</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><msup><mi>r</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>4</mn></mrow></msup><msup><mrow><mo mathvariant="italic"> </mo><mi mathvariant="italic">g</mi></mrow><mi mathvariant="italic">x</mi></msup><msup><mi>l</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mfrac><mn>3</mn><mn>2</mn></mfrac></mrow></msup></math> If the percentage errors found in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi><mo mathvariant="italic">,</mo><mo mathvariant="italic"> </mo><mi>m</mi><mo mathvariant="italic">,</mo><mo mathvariant="italic"> </mo><mi>r</mi><mo mathvariant="italic">,</mo><mo mathvariant="italic"> </mo><mi>l</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>g</mi></math> are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>18</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>1</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>4</mn></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>p</mi></math> respectively, then find the value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>p</mi></math>.
solution:
Δyy=2Δmm+4Δrr+xΔgg+32Δll

18=2(1)+4(0.5)+xp+32(4)

8=xp

By checking from options.

x=163, p=±32
